coredata.dc:title: Construction of CuInS2/C/TiO2 hierarchical tandem heterostructures with optimized CO2 photoreduction under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Wei
Zhang, Ling
Jiang, Hao
Li, Chunzhong
Hu, Yanjie
coredata.dc:description: The precise regulation of the nano-catalyst structure is a big challenge that limits the application of photocatalytic technology in CO2 reduction. Herein, to improve the utilization of sunlight, the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the surface catalytic reaction process, CuInS2/C/TiO2 (CIS/C/T) hierarchical tandem heterostructure have been rationally designed. Multiple in-situ characterizations and DFT simulations show that CQDs not only act as a co-catalyst, but also act as a bridge between TiO2 (T) and CuInS2 (CIS), which further attribute to the efficient separation and enrichment of photogenerated electron-hole pairs while effectively avoiding the recombination. In consideration of the excellent broad-spectrum absorption, the CO2-to-CO photoreduction performance of the optimal CIS/C/T reaches up to 7.73 μmol g-1h−1, about 3.16 times enhancement of C/T. Combined with in-situ DRIFTs, the intrinsic structure–activity relationship is pointed out, and extended to other similar TiO2-based catalytic systems (In2S3/C/TiO2). This work will provide a new universal strategy for the design and synthesis of high-performance nano-photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultra-fast flame synthesis
Hierarchical tandem heterostructure
CO2 photoreduction
Visible light
Structure–activity relationship

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the preparation process of CIS/C/T hierarchical tandem heterostructure materials. (b) The XRD patterns, (c) SEM image, (d) TEM image, (e) the locally enlarged TEM image and (f) the corresponding STEM-EDS mappings of CIS/C/T.
In-situ XPS spectra of CIS/C/T under different conditions (without light; with 420 nm or 365 nm light irradiation). (a) Ti 2p, (b) O 1 s, (c) S 2p, (d) In 3d, (e) Cu 2p and (f) C 1 s.

(a) The UV–vis DRS and (b) the corresponding Tauc plots; (c) the Mott-Schottky curves; (d) the transient photocurrent responses; (e) PL spectra and (f) time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy with 350 nm laser excitation for C/T, CIS and CIS/C/T.

(a) Comparison of the CO products conversion over 3 h and (b) the corresponding conversion rate histogram of the targeted samples. (c) The cycle activity test of photocatalytic CO2 reduction process over the optimal sample CIS/C/T; and (d) the control group experiment to eliminate the effects of systematic errors or irrelevant variables.

(a) In-situ DRIFT spectra and (b) the corresponding contour plots for CIS/C/T; (c) In-situ DRIFT spectra and (d) the corresponding contour plots for CIS; (e) In-situ DRIFT spectra and (f) the corresponding contour plots for C/T. The adsorption stage locates at the lower part of the orange curve and the reaction process with light locates at the upper part of the orange curve in (a, c, e). This orange curve is consistent with the dotted line across 60 min in (b, d, f).
Electron transfer mechanism diagram deduced based on the WF result with DFT simulation.
Calculation results of work function related data.

head.ce:title: Construction of CuInS2/C/TiO2 hierarchical tandem heterostructures with optimized CO2 photoreduction under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Environmental Friendly Materials Technical Service Platform, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Environmental Friendly Materials Technical Service Platform
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Environmental Friendly Materials Technical Service Platform, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science & Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The precise regulation of the nano-catalyst structure is a big challenge that limits the application of photocatalytic technology in CO2 reduction. Herein, to improve the utilization of sunlight, the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the surface catalytic reaction process, CuInS2/C/TiO2 (CIS/C/T) hierarchical tandem heterostructure have been rationally designed. Multiple in-situ characterizations and DFT simulations show that CQDs not only act as a co-catalyst, but also act as a bridge between TiO2 (T) and CuInS2 (CIS), which further attribute to the efficient separation and enrichment of photogenerated electron-hole pairs while effectively avoiding the recombination. In consideration of the excellent broad-spectrum absorption, the CO2-to-CO photoreduction performance of the optimal CIS/C/T reaches up to 7.73 μmol g-1h−1, about 3.16 times enhancement of C/T. Combined with in-situ DRIFTs, the intrinsic structure–activity relationship is pointed out, and extended to other similar TiO2-based catalytic systems (In2S3/C/TiO2). This work will provide a new universal strategy for the design and synthesis of high-performance nano-photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The reduction of CO2 into high value-added carbon-containing raw materials through advanced photocatalytic technology on particulate semiconductors is considered to be one of the most promising strategies to overcome global energy and climate change [1–5]. At present, the development of a catalyst system with high activity, stability and selectivity is one of the key challenges limiting the development of this technology [6–9]. In general, the factors that affect the photocatalytic efficiency are commonly thought to include the following three processes: the absorption of sunlight, the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the surface catalytic reaction process [10–14]. So far, a variety of catalysts have been developed and tried to solve one or more of the above problems, the most representative of which is the TiO2-based photocatalyst system [15–22]. But one of the biggest problems of TiO2 is that the band gap is relatively large, so the utilization rate of sunlight is concentrated in the ultraviolet light (UV) area with a very small proportion [23–25]. However, the energy provided by the visible light region occupies about half of the solar spectrum, so the development of semiconductors that can effectively use visible light to achieve high-efficiency catalysis is crucial. Therefore, scientists have made many attempts from the perspective of energy band regulation. Yin et al., [26] reported that a variety of new types of yellow, blue and black TiO2 can be obtained by creating different concentrations of oxygen vacancies on the surface of TiO2 nanocrystals, and their corresponding band gaps are gradually narrowing. Thus, the light response is extended from UV to visible light region, and high photocatalytic performance is achieved. However, a narrower band gap also means easier recombination of electron-hole pairs, which is not conducive to the photocatalytic process. Therefore, it has been proposed to use a suitable sulfide semiconductor with a smaller band gap, or composite with modified TiO2, to collect solar energy in a wide visible light range for efficient light conversion. However, it usually suffers from unstable material structure and slower interface reaction kinetics.

Inspired by the natural process of photosynthesis, researchers realized that two separate semiconductor light collectors could be combined to promote the release of carbon-containing materials driven by visible light. However, the traditional Z-scheme heterojunction often requires an aqueous redox medium to complete the cycle. This complex working condition makes the utilization efficiency of the effective photo-generated electrons participating in the reaction low [27,28]. Taking into consideration, the strategy of constructing an all-solid-state heterojunction can well solve the above-mentioned problems. As an emerging carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have unique optical and electronic properties, and are often used as promoters to be supported on semiconductors to build heterostructure systems [29–33]. However, most of the previous reports usually focus on the simple integration of CQDs with single or multiple semiconductor materials, and lack reasonable design and control of nanoscale architecture for the three key processes in the photocatalytic process mentioned above, and the in-depth structure-effectiveness relationship needs further exploration.

In this article, the CuInS2/C/TiO2 (CIS/C/T) hierarchical tandem heterostructure photocatalyst has been designed and synthesized, which greatly improves the utilization of visible light in the solar spectrum, thereby realizing high-efficiency photocatalytic efficiency. The catalyst bases on CQDs and C co-modified TiO2 (C/T) as the matrix, and closely surrounds the surface of a single C/T particle to build a CuInS2 (CIS) composite hierarchical structure. Among them, CQDs not only act as a co-catalyst, but also act as a bridge between T and CIS, integrating the two into a series system CIS/C/T, thus achieving high efficiency in promoting photogenerated electron-hole pairs separating and enriching, while the recombination is effectively avoided. Because of the excellent sunlight absorption capacity, the photocatalytic CO2-to-CO performance of the optimal CIS/C/T reaches 7.73 μmol g-1h−1, which is 3.16 times of the C/T, and shows excellent stability. In addition, multiple in-situ characterization methods and DFT simulations all pointed out that the electron transfer mechanism of the target material conforms to the characteristics of the tandem heterostructure. Moreover, combined with the results of in-situ DRIFTs, the in-depth structure–activity relationship was pointed out and extended to other similar catalytic systems (In2S3/C/TiO2). This work will provide a new case for the design and synthesis of high-performance particulate photocatalysts.

Firstly, the CQDs and lattice C co-modified TiO2 sample (C/T) was prepared by flame spray pyrolysis (FSP) method, which can be referred to previous work for detail [34]. Notably, CQDs are the research focus in this article. Briefly, a certain amount of tetrabutyl titanate (TBT, Adamas, AR) were dissolved in absolute ethanol (Ti precursor concentration keeps 0.5 M), then stirred for 5 min and ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min to form uniform and transparent Ti precursor solution. Subsequently, the precursor solution was transferred into reaction device through injection pump (at a feed rate of 5 mL min−1). The droplets were atomized by diffused oxygen (diffusion pressure: 0.15 MPa) when passed through the nozzle of the capillary. The atomized tiny droplets were burnt in the H2/Air (3.3 L min−1/16.7 L min−1) diffusion flame. The powder was completely collected on the glass fiber filter by a vacuum collecting device. The powder was post-processed in Ar at 450 °C for 1.5 h (at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1) to form a tightly connected C/T sample.

Then, this as-prepared C/T sample was compounded with different proportions of CuInS2 (CIS) through in-situ co-precipitation method. In detail, take 853 mg CuCl2·2H2O (Sinopharm, 99%), 1465 mg InCl3·4H2O (Aladdin, 100%), 480 mg S powder (Adamas, 99.999%), and dissolve them in 15 mL ethylene glycol (Aladdin, 99%), and stir for 10 min. Use a single-necked flask to measure 15 mL of triethylenetetramine (Macklin, 70%), quickly pour the mixed solution from the previous step into the single-necked flask, and then add 400 mg C/T. Transfer to an oil bath and react for 12 h at 450 rpm. Take out the precipitate and wash it 4 times with a mixed solution of ethanol and water, and dry at 60 °C overnight. The final product denoted as CIS/C/T. Keep the same conditions, the final product with 200 mg C/T addition was noted as CIS/C/T 2:1; and the final product with 800 mg C/T addition was noted as CIS/C/T 1:2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Talos F200 TEM) was used to observe the targeted CIS/C/T heterostructure and the contrasts. The lattice structure was characterized with the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Avance) with Cu-Kα filament that X-ray wavelength of 1.5406 Å. The chemical state of Ti 2p, O 1 s, S 2p, In 3d, Cu 2p and C1s were further studied by the in-situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (in-situ XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer) to show the electron transfer. The band gap of ST was detected by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS, U-3900H Spectrophotometer) within the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (in-situ DRIFTS) was obtained on the spectrometer (Tensor II FT-IR, Bruker) equipped with a reaction chamber. In a dark room, treat at 120 °C for 1 h in an Ar atmosphere to remove impurities and collect a spectrum as the reference curve; then pass the CO2 and H2O mixture for 60 min, and collect data once in an interval; Turn on the Xe lamp to irradiate for 60 min, and collect data every 10 min. The ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Xi) was adopted to figure out the work function (WF) of T, CQDs and CIS, which help DFT result prove the tandem heterostructure.

The performance testing was based on the all-glass automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar 6A, Beijing Perfectlight, China) equipped with an online gas chromatograph system (GC 2060, Shanghai Ruimin). Firstly, 10 mg targeted samples were dispersed in 0.5 mL DI water. After sonication for 10 min, the suspension was uniformly dispersed in the center area of the glass fiber filter (Φ = 47 mm). Then dried for 30 min at 60 °C. The treated as-prepared catalyst was placed in a quartz reactor (volume of 400 mL) together with 0.3 mL of DI water hanging on the container wall, and the system was repeatedly washed with CO2 gas (99.99%, Shangnong Gas, China) for 2 times. Finally, the test procedure began after the equilibrium was reached. Turn on the Xe lamp (PLS-SXE300D, Beijing Perfectlight, China), which is equipped with a visible light filter (λ > 420 nm). Data was collected once an hour during 3 h reaction period. The yield of gas products (CO, CH4 etc.) were determined and calibrated based on standard curves.

Fig. 1
a shows the synthesis schematic of the targeted sample. The C/T was first synthesized through a flame process, and the structural characteristics of C/T are clearly shown in our previous work [34]. Then, CIS/C/T hierarchical tandem heterostructures were prepared through an in-situ deposition process. Fig. 1 b shows that the main components in the targeted sample are T and CIS. In addition, the SEM images in Fig. 1 c show that the targeted sample is distributed in granular form, and the diameter of a single particle is about 200–300 nm. Furthermore, the TEM images in Fig. 1 d and the corresponding locally enlarged images in Fig. 1 e clearly show the information of anatase (101) crystal plane, rutile (110) crystal plane, and CuInS2 (004) crystal plane. This proves once again that TiO2 and CuInS2 stay in a bonded state. Meanwhile, STEM-HADDF image and corresponding STEM-EDS mappings are clearly shown in Fig. 1 f. Specifically, the signals of Ti and O elements are concentrated on the inside, while the signals of Cu, In, and S are relatively distributed on the outside. The mix images in Fig. S1 can more clearly reflect the spatial structure relationship between these two species. This result indicates that the targeted material is composed of a single T particle surface covered with CIS particles. Overall, the CIS/C/T particles is a hierarchical heterostructure nanosphere formed by stacking multiple T NPs @ CIS NPs and the above synthetic schematic is also drawn based on this result. Notably, such a stacked structure will provide a good channel for the reaction molecules or light to penetrate into the interior of the particles. In addition, CIS and CIS/C/T related synthesis and structural supplementary materials are shown in Figs. S2-S4 .

The study of electron transfer mechanism is the key to understanding the microstructure of photocatalytic materials. In order to explain the behavior of electrons, in-situ XPS was adopted to study the targeted material. The result of Ti 2p in Fig. 2
a shows that after irradiated 420 nm light, the characteristic peak Ti 2p3/2 shifted from low binding energy (LBE, 458.31 eV) to high binding energy (HBE, 458.78 eV). A higher binding energy indicates that the Ti atom in the targeted material loses electrons [35,36]. Similarly, the O 1 s result shown in Fig. 2 b is similar to the Ti 2p result, indicating that O also loses electrons. The results of combining Ti 2p and O 1 s prove that the T species is an electron donor. Meanwhile, the S 2p result in Fig. 2 c shows that its characteristic peak is shifted from LBE (160.56 eV) to HBE (161.18 eV), which also shows that the S atom in the target material also loses electrons. The same result can be found from In 3d shown in Fig. 2 d and Cu 2p shown in Fig. 2 e. Combining the results of S 2p, In 3d and Cu 2p, it proves that the CIS species is an electron donor. Notably, the C 1 s in Fig. 2 f shows the completely opposite result. The strongest peak is shifted from HBE (284.51 eV) to LBE (284.8 eV) with 420 nm light. The result shows that C acts as an electron acceptor is in this process, which enriches electrons from T and CIS. In order to further discuss whether stronger UV light will affect the above results. The 365 nm illumination results shown in Fig. 2 a-2e show that the change trend of binding energy is comparable to that of visible light, indicating that visible light is sufficient to excite the electron-hole pair separation of T and CIS. In addition, the HR-TEM image of Fig. S5 shows the use of light-deposited Au as a probe to visually prove that the photogenerated electron enrichment sites are between CIS and T. The above result will be as supplementary evidence together with the in-situ XPS results to show that the above electron transfer mechanism meets the characteristics of tandem heterojunction. Based on the above analysis, it is speculated that the targeted material will exhibit excellent performance as a novel tandem heterostructure system. This result will provide the necessary theoretical basis for the following structure–activity relationship research.

Further, the optical and photoelectrochemical properties of the targeted material were characterized. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) results in Fig. 3
a show that CIS has excellent absorption capacity of the entire solar spectrum, which greatly solve the problem of low utilization of visible light in C/T materials. Therefore, the targeted material CIS/C/T exhibits a very good visible light absorption efficiency, which is somewhere in between and close to CIS. Since C/T materials belong to indirect band gap materials and CIS belongs to direct band gap materials, after data transformation, [37] the corresponding Tauc plot curve obtained is shown in Fig. 3 b. The results show that the target material has a band gap width of 1.82 eV. In comparison, the band gap of CIS is narrower to 1.50 eV, while the band gap of C/T is much wider (2.74 eV). The proper band gap will help the effective transfer and utilization of electrons. Furthermore, in order to clarify the band structure of the targeted material, Fig. 3 c shows the Mott-Schottky test results. Spectrogram data show that the flat band potentials vasus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) are −0.045 V, −0.455 V, and −0.350 V for T, CIS, and CIS/C/T, respectively, which indicate that the formation of the CIS/C/T tandem heterostructure caused a slight change in the position of the flat belt. After processing, [38] the conduction bands (ECB) of the three groups of materials correspond to −0.245 eV, −0.655 eV, and −0.550 eV, respectively. The ECB result shows that the targeted sample could meet the thermodynamic requirements of CO2 reduction in the actual reaction process. Combining the above band gap width and ECB results, the valence band (EVB) of the targeted materials can be calculated as 2.495 eV, 0.845 eV, and 1.270 eV. In addition, the photocurrent test results in Fig. 3 d show that CIS/C/T has the strongest current intensity signal, followed by CIS, and C/T is the weakest, indicating that tandem heterostructures constructed by CIS and C/T can achieve synergistic signal enhancement effects. Meanwhile, in the photoluminescence spectra (PL) of Fig. 3 e, CIS/C/T show the weakest fluorescence intensity, indicating that the electron-hole pairs have the lowest degree of recombination. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) results in Fig. S6 reflect that the CIS/C/T structure has the smallest impedance. Based on the above analysis, the photo-generated carrier has a stronger migration ability in the targeted material structure, and the number of effective electrons that migrate to the surface of the material is greater than that of the control group. Notably, the more intuitive electronic lifetime data are provided in Fig. 3 f, which show that the lifetimes of C/T, CIS and CIS/C/T are 7.51 ns, 7.81 ns and 10.49 ns, respectively, and the original fitting parameters are also shown in Table S1. This result once again proves that the advantage of the CIS/C/T structure as the optimal targeted material is that it can generate more effective photo-generated electrons for the subsequent catalytic reaction process.

The comparison of the catalytic performance of the five photocatalytic materials is shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4 a shows that the CO2-to-CO photoreduction activity of the best CIS/C/T sample can reach 22.51 μmol g−1 over 3 h. As compared, C/T is 7.21 μmol g−1 and STO is 0. Meanwhile, Fig. S7 shows that the CO2-to-CH4 photoreduction activity of all catalysts other than CIS is close to 0. Fig. 4 b shows the corresponding conversion rate histogram of the targeted samples. The result can more intuitively show that the CO rate of CIS/C/T is 7.73 μmol g-1h−1, which is 3.16 times that of the control sample C/T (2.45 μmol g-1h−1). In addition, the results show that when the ratio of CIS and C/T is adjusted, the catalytic efficiency will be reduced. Specifically, CIS/C/T 800 drops to 4.10 μmol g-1h−1, and CIS/C/T 200 drops to 3.81 μmol g-1h−1. Therefore, it can be inferred that the inherent reason why the optimal targeted structure can improve the performance so significantly is to construct a tandem heterojunction electron transfer mechanism, which allows the electrons to flow in an orderly and directional direction, so as to greatly increase the number of effective electrons participated in the catalytic reaction process. Meanwhile, this kind of synergistic effect reaches the best under the dosage of 400 mg C/T. In addition, Fig. S8 shows the performance of pure TiO2. In order to verify the scientificity of the results, the cycle activity test results in Fig. 4 c show that the performance stability of the same group of CIS/C/T samples in three cycles is very good, with almost no performance degradation. Moreover, Figs. S9-S10 show that the structure and chemical properties of the catalyst before and after the reaction are stable. In addition, we also conducted 4 sets of control experiments on the optimized CIS/C/T samples in order to eliminate the systematic error, which is shown in Fig. 4 d. The results point that the instrument has a completely negligible error range and the test results are reliable.

In order to clarify the mechanism of the reaction process, we carried out the analysis of in-situ DRIFTs for CIS/C/T, CIS and C/T samples. Notably, the spectra are drawn on two different scales for better view, but also performed normalization in contour plots for better comparison. Fig. 5
a shows the test results of the CIS/C/T sample in the adsorption stage (the lower part of the orange curve) and the reaction process with light (the upper part of the orange curve). In the adsorption stage, in addition to the obvious absorption peak of H2O at 1642 cm−1, bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2-, 1614 cm−1), formate (HCOO–, 1331 cm−1), hydrogen carbonate (HCO3 –, 1208 cm−1) and multiple peaks in characteristic carbonate and formate groups (1100–1200 cm−1) are also observed. In particular, the targeted sample also appear obvious strong adsorption signals of CO2 species (b-CO2, 1562 cm−1) and CO2 – signals (1687 cm−1, 1247 cm−1) [39–43]. Taken together, the signal intensity of the above various peaks are all at a relatively high level, indicating that the targeted material has active CO2 adsorption and conversion capabilities. Over time, b-CO3 2- gradually become the strongest main peak. When enters the reaction process with light, the intensity of the H2O peak continues to increase, meanwhile, the concentration of HCOO– continues to increase too. Combined with Fig. 4 b, it can be more clearly shown that HCOO– at 1779 cm−1 has accumulated significantly, indicating that HCOO– is one of the key species driving the photocatalytic CO2 conversion process. Our previous studies pointed out that the accumulation of HCOO– intermediates will contribute to the conversion efficiency of CO2-to-CO [44]. In addition, there is a significant peak signal enhancement near 1495 cm−1 (shown more intuitively in Fig. 5 b), which is due to the strong adsorption of CO on the surface of the material [45]. The above results reflect to some extent the main reason why CO is a characteristic product. Fig. 4 b more clearly shows that after 60 min (that is, light on), all the above peaks represented by HCOO– have obvious enhancements. The rapid accumulation of intermediate state matter indicates that light will be an effective driving force. It helps to accelerate the production of photogenerated electrons to participate in the process of chemical reactions, thereby promoting the kinetic process of CO2 reduction.

Compared with the CIS/C/T sample, the CIS sample of Fig. 5 c shows a general weakening of the signal of each species from the beginning of the adsorption stage. Meanwhile, the disappearance of the CO2 – species signal also indicates the decline of CIS’s ability to capture CO2 and transport electrons. In the light phase, the signals of all species have accumulated to a certain extent, but the gap with the CIS/C/T samples has not been improved. This result indicates that CIS has its own disadvantage in kinetics. However, under the same conditions, the peak signal of HCOO– (1384 cm−1, 1331 cm−1) can still be detected, indicating that HCOO– is one of the key species driving the photocatalytic CO2 conversion process in CIS samples. In addition, the strength of CO adsorption has been weakened to a certain extent, thus contributing to the further conversion of CO-to-CH4, indicating that CH4 will replace CO as a characteristic product. The above results can also be visually displayed from the lighter color of the area corresponding to the HCOO– and CO product adsorption peaks in Fig. 5 d.

The change trend in C/T shown in Fig. 5 e is similar to that of the CIS sample, but the overall strength is weaker. From Fig. 5 f, it can be found that the overall blue area goes up over time, indicating that the C/T sample is less sensitive to CO2 photoreduction than others. Specifically, the main peaks in the adsorption stage are mainly b-HCO3 - at 1623 cm−1 and b-CO3 2- at 1408 cm−1. With the introduction of light, HCOO– begins to accumulate, and the product signal of CO begin to be detected. But different from the above two groups, the signal strength of CO is even weaker. The further reduced CO adsorption intensity indicates that the reaction kinetics of the CO2-to-CO process is relatively slow, and the CO-to-CH4 reaction process cannot be supported to continue. Therefore, the final performance test results show that compared with CIS/C/T, its apparent CO output was significantly reduced; and compared with CIS, its characteristic product was CO. The above analysis process shows that the reaction mechanism and performance test results maintain good consistency. In addition, all relevant data analysis results of the full spectrum (4000–600 cm−1) are provided in Figs. S11-S12 .

To further understand the structure–activity relationship. We conducted density functional theory (DFT) calculations for the targeted material. The results in Fig. 6 show that the work function (WF) of carbon-doped anatase TiO2 (101) crystal plane is 5.02 eV, the WF of CIS (012) is 4.73 eV, and the WF of CQDs composed of 257 carbon atoms is the largest 5.39 eV. According to the relative relationship between Fermi level (EF), vacuum level (Evac) and WF, Fig. 6 a is obtained, which clearly shows that the flow of electrons is from CIS and T to CQDs, and CQDs will act as a collector of electrons to further reduce the CO2 to CO with solar energy. Fig. 6 b-6c show the above DFT models of T, CQDs and CIS respectively. In addition, detailed calculation data are shown in Table 1 . Interestingly, the size of the CQDs will cause a change in the WF, and a volcanic image can be obtained. Among them, the WF of CQDs (∼1.34 nm) composed of 257 carbon atoms is the largest. In addition to the theoretical simulation of DFT, the UPS was also adopted to test the WF for the targeted materials. The results in Fig. S13 are well consistent with the above conclusions. Herein, the tandem heterostructure in the targeted CIS/C/T system is proved from both experimental and theoretical perspectives. Moreover, the structure–activity relationship between the generation of tandem heterostructures and the enhanced photoreduction performance is positively correlated.

In order to verify the universality of the system, we also conducted research on the In2S3/C/TiO2 system, and the relevant results are shown in Figs. S14-S15 . Related results such as in-situ XPS also show that this strategy of constructing tandem heterostructures is universal, that is, it can be extended from the CIS/C/T discussed in this work to other structural systems that are compounded with C/T. What is more, this result also provides a reference for the preparation of a new type of high-efficiency tandem heterojunction hierarchical structure based on the flame process.

Herein, CIS/C/T hierarchical tandem heterostructure has been rationally designed and synthesized, which improves the low utilization rate of sunlight, the low efficiency of separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and poor surface catalytic reaction process in TiO2. Multiple in-situ characterizations and DFT simulations show that the CQDs in the targeted material not only act as a promoter, but also act as a bridge between T and CIS, which further realize the efficient separation and enrichment of photogenerated electron-hole pairs while effectively avoiding the probability of mutual recombination. Owing to the excellent broad-spectrum absorption capacity, the photocatalytic CO2-to-CO performance of the optimal CIS/C/T reaches 7.73 μmol g-1h−1 with excellent stability, which is 3.16 times of the control sample C/T. What is more, combined with in-situ DRIFTs, pointed out the structure–activity relationship between the material structure and the highly selective conversion of CO2-to-CO, and successfully extend it to other similar TiO2-based catalytic systems (In2S3/C/TiO2). This work will provide a new universal case for the design and synthesis of high-performance nano-photocatalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978088, 91534202, 51673063), Shanghai Technology Research Leader (20XD1433600), the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutes of High Learning, the Basic Research Program of Shanghai (17JC1402300), the Shanghai City Board of education research and innovation project, and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201718002). Additional support was provided by Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.133679.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic CO2 conversion: what can we learn from conventional COx hydrogenation?

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. T. Kong, Y. W. Jiang, Y. J. Xiong, Photocatalytic CO2 conversion: What can we learn from conventional COx hydrogenation? Chem. Soc. Rev. 49 (2020) 6579-6591.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Filling metal–organic framework mesopores with TiO2 for CO2 photoreduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Jiang, X. H. Xu, Y. H. Ma, H. S. Cho, D. Ding, C. Wang, J. Wu, P. Oleynikov, M. Jia, J. Cheng, Y. Zhou, O. Terasaki, T. Y. Peng, L. Zan, H. X. Deng, Filling metal–organic framework mesopores with TiO2 for CO2 photoreduction. Nature 58 (2020) 549–554.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light-driven methane formation from CO2 with a molecular iron catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Rao, L. C. Schmidt, J. Bonin, M. Robert, Visible-light-driven methane formation from CO2 with a molecular iron catalyst. Nature 548 (2017) 74–77.
C. Hu, S. C. Tu, N. Tian, T. Y. Ma, Y. H. Zhang, H. W. Huang, Photocatalysis Enhanced by External Fields. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 60 (2021) 16309–16328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar-driven metal halide perovskite photocatalysis: design, stability, and performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. W. Huang, B. P. Pradhan, J. Hofkens, M. B. J. Roeffaers, J. A. Steele, Solar-Driven Metal Halide Perovskite Photocatalysis: Design, Stability, and Performance. ACS Energy Lett. 5 (2020) 1107−1123.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: F. Zhang, Y. H. Li, M. Y. Qi, Z. R. Tang, Y. J. Xu, Boosting the activity and stability of Ag-Cu2O/ZnO nanorods for photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Appl. Catal., B 268 (2020) 118380.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cocatalysts in semiconductor-based photocatalytic CO2 reduction: achievements, challenges, and opportunities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. R. Ran, M. Jaroniec, S. Z. Qiao, Cocatalysts in Semiconductor-based Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction: Achievements, Challenges, and Opportunities. Adv. Mater. 30 (2018) 1704649.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction with suppressing H2 evolution via Pt cocatalyst and surface SiO2 coating

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. K. Wu, Y. H. Li, M. Y. Qi, Q. Lin, Y. J. Xu, Enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction with suppressing H2 evolution via Pt cocatalyst and surface SiO2 coating. Appl. Catal., B 278 (2020) 119267.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4 over Pt/In2O3: Significant role of hydrogen adatom

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. B. Wang, J. Zhao, Y. X. Li, C. Y. Wang, Selective photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4 over Pt/In2O3: Significant role of hydrogen adatom. Appl. Catal., B 226 (2018) 544-553.
C. P. Xu, P. R. Anusuyadevi, C. Aymonier, R. Luque, S. Marre, Nanostructured materials for photocatalysis. Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 3868-3902.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar synthesis: prospects in visible light photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. M. Schultz, T. P. Yoon, Solar Synthesis: Prospects in Visible Light Photocatalysis. Science 343 (2014) 1239176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer photocatalysts for solar-to-chemical energy conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Banerjee, F. Podjaski, J. Kröger, B. P. Biswal, B. V. Lotsch, Polymer photocatalysts for solar-to-chemical energy conversion. Nat. Rev. Mater. 6 (2021) 168–190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Z-scheme photocatalytic CO2 conversion on three-dimensional BiVO4/carbon-coated Cu2O nanowire arrays under visible light

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Kim, K. M. Cho, A. Al-Saggaf, I. Gereige, H. T. Jung, Z‑scheme Photocatalytic CO2 Conversion on Three-Dimensional BiVO4/Carbon-Coated Cu2O Nanowire Arrays under Visible Light. ACS Catal. 8 (2018), 4170−4177.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Van der Waals heterojunction for selective visible-light-driven photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, B. J. Huang, Q. Guo, S. Guo, Z. K. Peng, J. Liu, Q. Y. Tian, Y. P. Yang, Q. Xu, Z. Y. Liu, B. Liu, Van der Waals heterojunction for selective visible-light-driven photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Appl. Catal., B 284 (2021) 119733.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Improving selectivity and activity of CO2 reduction photocatalysts with oxygen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kreft, R. Schoch, J. Schneidewind, Jabor Rabeah, E. V. Kondratenko, V. A. Kondratenko, H. Junge, M. Bauer, S. Wohlrab, M. Beller, Improving Selectivity and Activity of CO2 Reduction Photocatalysts with Oxygen. Chem 5 (2019) 1818-1833.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor-covalent organic framework Z-scheme heterojunctions for artificial photosynthesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Q. Lan, M. Zhang, M. Lu, Z. L. Lang, J. Liu, M. Liu, J. N. Chang, L.Y. Li, L. J. Shang, M. Wang, S. L. Li, Y. Q. Lan, Semiconductor-Covalent Organic Framework Z-scheme Heterojunctions for Artificial Photosynthesis. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 6500-6506.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward high-value hydrocarbon generation by photocatalytic reduction of CO2 in water vapor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. X. Li, B. B. Wang, Y. T. Si, F. Xue, J. C. Zhou, Y. J. Lu, M. C. Liu, Toward High-Value Hydrocarbon Generation by Photocatalytic Reduction of CO2 in Water Vapor. ACS Catal. 9 (2019) 5590–5602.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. A. Rawool, K. K. Yadav, V. Polshettiwar, Defective TiO2 for photocatalytic CO2 conversion to fuels and chemicals. Chem. Sci. 12 (2021) 12 4267.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An unprecedent hydride transfer pathway for selective photocatalytic reduction of CO2 to formic acid on TiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. N. Zhang, Y. F. Li, J. Z. Wang, N. N. Wu, H. Sheng, C. C. Chen, J. C. Zhao, An Unprecedent Hydride Transfer Pathway for Selective Photocatalytic Reduction of CO2 to Formic Acid on TiO2. Appl. Catal., B 284 (2021) 119692.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances on TiO2-based photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kreft, D. Wei, H. Junge, Matthias Beller, Recent advances on TiO2-based photocatalytic CO2 reduction, EnergyChem 2 (2020), 100044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of large size graphene and Ti-MWCNTs/ large size graphene composites: their photocatalytic properties and potential application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Ullah, W.-C. Oh, Fabrication of large size graphene and Ti-MWCNTs/ large size graphene composites: their photocatalytic properties and potential application. Sci. Rep. 5 (2015), 14242.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into methanol over Cu–Ni–TiO2 supported on SiO2-Al2O3 catalyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Duangkaew, N. Suparee, P. Athikaphan, A. Neramittagapong, S. Neramittagapong, Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into methanol over Cu–Ni–TiO2 supported on SiO2-Al2O3 catalyst. Energy Rep. 6 (2020) 1157–1161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bi quantum dots obtained via in situ photodeposition method as A new photocatalytic CO2 reduction cocatalyst instead of noble metals: borrowing redox conversion between Bi2O3 and Bi

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. H. Yang, W. K. Miao, Z. M. Yuan, Z. Y. Jiang, B. B. Huang, P. Wang, J. C. Chen, Bi Quantum Dots Obtained via in Situ Photodeposition Method as A New Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction Cocatalyst Instead of Noble Metals: Borrowing Redox Conversion Between Bi2O3 and Bi. Appl. Catal., B 237 (2018) 302-308.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic effect of Cu single atoms and Au−Cu alloy nanoparticles on TiO2 for efficient CO2 photoreduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Y. Yu, X. A. Dong, P. Chen, Q. Geng, H. Wang, J. Y. Li, Y. Zhou, Fan Dong, Synergistic Effect of Cu Single Atoms and Au−Cu Alloy Nanoparticles on TiO2 for Efficient CO2 Photoreduction. ACS Nano (2021) DOI: 10.1021/acsnano.1c03961.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient photocatalysts of TiO2 nanocrystals-supported PtRu alloy nanoparticles for CO2 reduction with H2O: synergistic effect of Pt-Ru

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. C. Wei, X. X. Wu, Y. L. Zhao, L. Wang, Z. Zhao, X. T. Huang, J. Liu, J. M. Li, Efficient photocatalysts of TiO2 nanocrystals-supported PtRu alloy nanoparticles for CO2 reduction with H2O: Synergistic effect of Pt-Ru. Appl. Catal., B 236 (2018) 445–457.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogenated blue titania for efficient solar-to-chemical conversions: preparation, characterization, and reaction mechanism of CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. H. Yin, X. Y. Huang, T. Y. Chen, W. Zhao, Q. Y. Bi, J. Xu, Y. F. Han, F. Q. Huang, Hydrogenated Blue Titania for Efficient Solar-to-Chemical Conversions: Preparation, Characterization, and Reaction Mechanism of CO2 Reduction. ACS Catal. 8 (2018) 1009–1017.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Q. L. Xu, L. Y. Zhang, B. Cheng, J. J. Fan, J. G. Yu, S-Scheme Heterojunction Photocatalyst. Chem 6 (2020) 1543-1559.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on TiO2-based Z-scheme photocatalysts

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Z. Qi, B. Cheng, J. G. Yu, W. Ho, A review on TiO2‐based Z‐scheme photocatalysts. Chinese J. Catal. 38 (2017) 1936–1955.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots as photosensitisers for solar-driven catalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. A. M. Hutton, B. C. M. Martindale and E. Reisner, Carbon dots as photosensitisers for solar-driven catalysis. Chem. Soc. Rev. 46 (2017) 6111-6123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smart utilization of carbon dots in semiconductor photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. J. Yu, R. Shi, Y. F. Zhao, G. I. N. Waterhouse, L. Z. Wu, C. H. Tung, T. R. Zhang, Smart Utilization of Carbon Dots in Semiconductor Photocatalysis. Adv. Mater. 28 (2016) 9454-9477.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloidal quantum dot ligand engineering for high performance solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. L. Wang, Y. Q. Shang, P. Kanjanaboos, W. J. Zhou, Z. J. Ning, E. H. Sargent, Colloidal Quantum Dot Ligand Engineering for High Performance Solar Cells. Energy Environ. Sci. 9 (2016) 1130-1143.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Harnessing Vis–NIR broad spectrum for photocatalytic CO2 reduction over carbon quantum dots-decorated ultrathin Bi2WO6 nanosheets

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Y. Kong, W. L. Tan, B. J. Ng, S. P. Chai, A. R. Mohamed, Harnessing Vis–NIR broad spectrum for photocatalytic CO2 reduction over carbon quantum dots-decorated ultrathin Bi2WO6 nanosheets. Nano Res. 10 (2017) 1720–1731.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel ternary photocatalyst of single atom-dispersed silver and carbon quantum dots co-loaded with ultrathin g-C3N4 for broad spectrum photocatalytic degradation of naproxen

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. L. Wang, Y. F. Wang, Y. P. Feng, Y. Q. Zeng, Z. J. Xie, Q. X. Zhang, Y. H. Su, P. Chen, Y. Liu, K. Yao, W. Y. Lv, G. G. Liu, Novel ternary photocatalyst of single atom-dispersed silver and carbon quantum dots co-loaded with ultrathin g-C3N4 for broad spectrum photocatalytic degradation of naproxen. Appl. Catal., B 221 (2018) 510-520.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flame process constructing CQDs/TiO2-C heterostructure with novel electron transfer channel between internal and external carbon species

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Bi, Y. J. Hu, H. Jiang, J. Lei, X. Y. Wan, L. Zhang, C. Z. Li, Flame process constructing CQDs/TiO2-C heterostructure with novel electron transfer channel between internal and external carbon species. Combust. Flame 228 (2021) 163–172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ grown monolayer N-doped graphene on CdS hollow spheres with seamless contact for photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. B. Bie, B. C. Zhu, F. Y. Xu, L. Y. Zhang, J. G. Yu, In Situ Grown Monolayer N-Doped Graphene on CdS Hollow Spheres with Seamless Contact for Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction. Adv. Mater. 31 (2019) 1902868.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: All-solid-state Z-scheme a-Fe2O3/Amine- RGO/CsPbBr 3 hybrids for visible-light-driven photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jiang, J. F. Liao, H. Y. Chen, H. H. Zhang, J. Y. Li, X. D. Wang, and D. B. Kuang, All-Solid-State Z-Scheme a-Fe2O3/Amine- RGO/CsPbBr3 Hybrids for Visible-Light-Driven Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction. Chem 6 (2020) 1–15.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The facile fabrication of novel visible-light-driven Z-scheme CuInS2/Bi2WO6 heterojunction with intimate interface contact by in situ hydrothermal growth strategy for extraordinary photocatalytic performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Y. Lu, W. J. Che, X. F. Hu, Y. Wang, A. T. Zhang, F. Deng, S. L. Luo, D. D. Dionysiou, The facile fabrication of novel visible-light-driven Z-scheme CuInS2/Bi2WO6 heterojunction with intimate interface contact by in situ hydrothermal growth strategy for extraordinary photocatalytic performance. Chem. Eng. J. 356 (2019) 819-829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver phosphate-based Z-Scheme photocatalytic system with superior sunlight photocatalytic activities and anti-photocorrosion performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Cai, Y. T. Liu, L. L. Wang, S. Q. Zhang, Y. X. Zeng, J. L. Yuan, J. H. Ma, W. Y. Dong, C. B. Liu, S. L. Luo, Silver phosphate-based Z-Scheme photocatalytic system with superior sunlight photocatalytic activities and anti-photocorrosion performance. Appl. Catal., B 208 (2017) 1-13.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dielectric and microwave absorption properties of ZrB2/Al2O3 composite ceramics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. L. Su, X. H. He, J. Xu, J. B. Wang, Y. H. Qu, C. Fu, Y. L. Wang, Dielectric and microwave absorption properties of ZrB2/Al2O3 composite ceramics. J. Mater. Sci.: Mater. Electron. 30 (2019) 2630–2637.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CuInS2 sensitized TiO2 hybrid nanofibers for improved photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Y. Xu, J. J. Zhang, B. C. Zhu, J. G. Yu, J. S. Xu, CuInS2 sensitized TiO2 hybrid nanofibers for improved photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Appl. Catal., B 230 (2018) 194-202.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold-copper nanoalloys supported on TiO2 as photocatalysts for CO2 reduction by water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Neatu, J. A. Macia-Agullo, P. Concepción, H. Garcia, Gold-copper nanoalloys supported on TiO2 as photocatalysts for CO2 reduction by water. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 136 (2014) 15969–15976.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advantageous role of Ir0 supported on TiO2 nanosheets in photocatalytic CO2 reduction to CH4: fast electron transfer and rich surface hydroxyl groups

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. L. Tang, Z. Q. Wang, W. X. Zou, H. Y. Guo, Y. C. Wu, Y. Pu, Q. Tong, H. Q. Wan, X. R. Gu, L. Dong, J. F. Rong, Y. W. Chen, Advantageous Role of Ir0 Supported on TiO2 Nanosheets in Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction to CH4: Fast Electron Transfer and Rich Surface Hydroxyl Groups. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (2021) 6219−6228.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface phases of TiO2 nanoparticles studied by UV raman spectroscopy and FT-IR spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. G. Su, J. Zhang, Z. C. Feng, T. Chen, P. L. Ying, C. Li, Surface Phases of TiO2 Nanoparticles Studied by UV Raman Spectroscopy and FT-IR Spectroscopy. J. Phys. Chem. C 112 (2008) 7710–7716.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-fast construction of plaque-like Li2TiO3/TiO2 heterostructure for efficient gas-solid phase CO2 photoreduction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Bi, Y. J. Hu, N. Jiang, L. Zhang, H. Jiang, X. Zhao, C. Y. Wang, C. Z. Li, Ultra-fast construction of plaque-like Li2TiO3/TiO2 heterostructure for efficient gas-solid phase CO2 photoreduction. Appl. Catal., B 269 (2020) 118810.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photocatalytic CO2 reduction with H2O on TiO2 nanocrystals: comparison of anatase, rutile, and brookite polymorphs and exploration of surface chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. J. Liu, H. L. Zhao, J. Andino, Y. Li, Photocatalytic CO2 Reduction with H2O on TiO2 Nanocrystals: Comparison of Anatase, Rutile, and Brookite Polymorphs and Exploration of Surface Chemistry. ACS Catal. 2 (2012) 1817–1828.
